1: Pursue love (diōkete tēn agapēn): the verb diōkō means to pursue, chase, even persecute — the same verb used for persecution in 1 Corinthians 15:9. Paul uses it to describe the urgency of pursuing love and the eagerness with which spiritual gifts (especially prophecy) should be desired.
2: Speaking in tongues to God, not to people: the glossolalist speaks mysteries (mystēria) in the Spirit — the content is addressed upward (to God) rather than horizontally (to the assembly). This is an important distinction for understanding the gift: it is real Spirit-enabled communication with God, not fake speech.
3: Prophecy for edification, exhortation, comfort: the three functions of prophecy — oikodomē (building up, construction), paraklēsis (exhortation, encouragement, the same root as the Holy Spirit's name Paraclete), and paramythia (consolation, comfort) — are all horizontal, community-directed functions. This is why prophecy is superior in the assembly context.
4: The one who speaks in tongues builds up himself: the edification of the individual (heauton oikodomeō) is not dismissed but subordinated. Paul affirms the personal value of tongues (1 Corinthians 14:18 I thank God I speak in tongues more than all of you) while arguing that the assembly's corporate edification takes priority.
5: I wish you all spoke in tongues: Paul expresses a genuine desire for all to have the gift, showing he does not denigrate tongues. The comparative (mallon de hina prophēteuēte — but even more that you prophesy) is the key: prophecy is relatively more valuable for the assembly's purpose.
6: Revelation, knowledge, prophecy, or teaching: the four forms of intelligible Spirit-communication that benefit the assembly. All four involve conveying content that can be understood and received — the criterion is comprehensibility.
7: Flute and harp giving indistinct notes: the musical illustration was accessible to any Corinthian who had attended civic festivals, theater, or athletic competitions, all of which featured instrumental music. An instrument that played only random notes without melody communicated nothing.
8: The uncertain trumpet sound: the military analogy of the trumpet (salpinx) was universally understood in the Roman empire. The salpinx sounded the advance, retreat, and different battle maneuvers — each call had a specific meaning. An unclear trumpet call was potentially fatal in battle.
9: Intelligible words with the tongue: the argument from analogy lands on the specific point: speaking in tongues without interpretation is like an indistinct trumpet — the hearer cannot respond appropriately because the message is inaccessible.
10: Many kinds of voices (phōnai) in the world: Paul grants the existence of many languages (phōnai, sounds, voices) — each meaningful to its native speakers. The diversity of human languages was noted by ancient writers (Josephus, Antiquities 1.5.1; Philo, On the Confusion of Tongues).
11: A foreigner (barbaros) to the speaker: barbaros was the Greek term for anyone whose language sounded like babble (bar-bar) to Greek ears. The foreigner analogy is pointed: the tongues-speaker without interpretation is reducing the assembly to a crowd of mutual barbarians, unable to communicate.
12: Seek to excel in building up the church: the Corinthians' zeal for pneumatika (spiritual manifestations) is acknowledged and redirected. The same energy should be applied to gifts that build up (oikodomē) the ekklēsia — the civic-assembly term now applied to the gathered church.
13: Pray to interpret: the tongue-speaker should pray for the gift of interpretation (diermēneuō, to translate/explain across language) so that their private communion with God can become public edification for the assembly.
14: My spirit prays but my mind is unfruitful: pneuma and nous (spirit and mind) in distinction — in tongues the spirit (the deep center of the person animated by the Holy Spirit) prays, but the nous (rational faculty) contributes nothing. The nous remains akarpos (without fruit, unproductive) for the purpose of communal edification.
15: Pray with the spirit and with the mind also: the both/and solution — Paul does not advocate abandoning tongues but insisting that mind-involving intelligible prayer and song accompany or complement Spirit-prayer. The synagogue practice of intelligible liturgical prayer informs this instinct.
16: The one in the position of an outsider (idiōtēs): the idiōtēs in Corinthian context was probably the uninitiated visitor or the believer without the gift of tongues — someone who occupies the layperson's position relative to the specialist. In Greek civic life, idiōtēs was the private citizen as opposed to the public official.
17: You are giving thanks well enough but the other person is not built up: the eucharistia (thanksgiving, giving thanks) in tongues may be genuine and beautiful, but its benefit is limited to the one praying. Community discernment requires an evaluative standard beyond individual sincerity.
18: I speak in tongues more than all of you: Paul's remarkable disclosure — he has the gift of tongues more abundantly than any Corinthian. This disarms the argument that his preference for prophecy stems from envy or deficiency. He speaks from experience, not ignorance.
19: Five words with the mind vs. ten thousand in tongues: the numerical contrast (pente logous kata noun vs. myrioys en glōssē) dramatizes the principle. The number 10,000 (myrioi) was the Greek word for an uncountable multitude — the contrast between meaningful speech and unmeasurable meaningless speech is stark.
20: Do not be children in your thinking: the contrast between nēpioi (infants, childlike) in thinking and teleios (mature, complete) in thinking echoes the wisdom tradition. Sirach 1 Corinthians 3:9-11 and Philo use similar contrasts. The Corinthians' attraction to spectacular gifts reflects an immature evaluation of spiritual worth.
21: Citing Isaiah 28:11-12: Paul cites the LXX of Isaiah 28 (with other lips and another tongue God will speak to this people). In context, Isaiah prophesied Assyrian invasion as God's judgment on an unbelieving Israel — the foreign tongue of the Assyrian conqueror was a sign of judgment. Paul applies this typologically: tongues in the assembly function as a sign for unbelievers (a sign of divine presence that, without interpretation, judges rather than converts).
22: Tongues as a sign for unbelievers, prophecy for believers: Paul's distinction is counterintuitive at first reading but follows from Isaiah 28. Tongues without interpretation confront the unbeliever with an incomprehensible divine speech (a sign of judgment); prophecy speaks the divine word intelligibly for those who already believe.
23: If the whole church comes together and all speak in tongues: the scenario Paul imagines — an outsider (idiōtēs) or unbeliever entering an assembly of unintelligible ecstatic speech — would result in the verdict that the Christians are out of their minds (mainomai, the same word used of Dionysiac frenzy). The church's public witness is at stake.
24: If all prophesy and an unbeliever enters: the contrasting scenario — intelligible prophetic speech convicts (elenchō, convict, expose), examines (anakrinetai), reveals the secrets of the heart, causing the visitor to fall on his face, worship God, and declare that God is truly among you. The missionary and evangelistic value of intelligible worship is the practical argument for prophecy.
25: God is truly among you (ontōs en hymin estin): the declaration of the converted visitor echoes OT theophanies — the sense of actual divine presence manifested in the assembly. This is the goal of corporate worship: an encounter with the living God that transforms.
26: When you come together, each one has a psalm, a teaching, a revelation, a tongue, an interpretation: the description of an early Christian assembly — participatory, diverse, Spirit-animated. This is not a structured liturgy but a Spirit-ordered gathering where multiple contributions are expected and evaluated.
27: Two or at most three in tongues, and each in turn: Paul imposes structure on the spontaneous gift. The limitation to two or three, the requirement of sequential (not simultaneous) speech, and the requirement of interpretation bring order without suppressing the gift.
28: If there is no interpreter, let the tongue-speaker be silent: the silence is not permanent but situational — without the interpreter function, tongues cannot serve the assembly's edification. The silent tongue-speaker may still pray inwardly (laleito de heautō kai tō theō — let him speak to himself and to God).
29: Two or three prophets, and let the others weigh what is said: the same structural limitation applies to prophecy. The diakrinetōsan (discern, judge, weigh) is a communal function — the assembly evaluates prophetic utterances rather than receiving them uncritically. This prophetic testing was a feature of Jewish prophetic practice (Deuteronomy 18:21-22; 1 John 4:1).
30: If a revelation comes to someone else sitting: the dynamic assembly allows for spontaneous prophetic utterance during the meeting. The seated prophet may receive a revelation; the standing prophet yields to allow the new word to be spoken. The process is orderly but flexible.
31: All may prophesy one by one: the democratic access to prophetic speech (one by one, kata hena) reflects the Joel 2:28 promise (your sons and daughters will prophesy). Paul's assembly is not monopolized by a single charismatic leader.
32: The spirits of prophets are subject to the prophets: the pneumata tōn prophētōn (the spiritual endowments/impulses of prophets) are under the prophets' own self-control. This directly counters the claim that ecstatic possession inevitably overrides self-control — a claim made for the Delphic Pythia and Dionysiac prophets.
33: God is not a God of disorder (akatastasia) but of peace (eirēnē): the theological anchor for the entire organizational discussion. Akatastasia was the term for political and social upheaval in Hellenistic usage (Josephus, Wars 4.3.9). The church's orderly worship reflects the character of its God.
34: Women to be silent in the churches: this instruction stands in apparent tension with 1 Corinthians 11:5 (women pray and prophesy). The silence commanded here may be specific — perhaps the practice of publicly questioning or challenging prophetic utterances during the assembly, which would violate the honor of the public space. Some manuscripts place 1 Corinthians 14:34-35 after 1 Corinthians 14:40, suggesting possible textual movement in the transmission.
35: If they want to learn something, let them ask their husbands at home: the home as the appropriate educational venue for women's questions reflects the Jewish synagogue practice where women and men were separated, and formal instruction was the domain of the male household head. Paul may be addressing a specific disruptive behavior in the Corinthian assembly.
36: Did the word of God originate with you? — the sharp rhetorical rebuke suggests that some Corinthians were treating their local practices as the standard for all churches. The twin questions challenge the Corinthian pretension to set the norms of Christian worship.
37: If anyone thinks he is a prophet or spiritual: those who claim prophetic or spiritual status must demonstrate it by recognizing that Paul's instructions are a command of the Lord (entolē kyriou). The test of genuine spiritual discernment is submission to apostolic authority.
38: If anyone does not recognize this, he is not recognized: the consequence of refusing Paul's teaching is not being recognized — by God, by the community, as genuinely prophetic. The passive agnoeisthai (to be unknown/unrecognized) may be a divine passive.
39: Earnestly desire to prophesy and do not forbid speaking in tongues: the balanced conclusion — prophecy is to be pursued eagerly; tongues are not to be suppressed. Paul does not eliminate tongues from the assembly but regulates them in the service of communal edification.
40: All things should be done decently and in order (euschēmonōs kai kata taxin): euschēmonōs (becomingly, properly, in a fitting manner) was a term of social propriety in Hellenistic culture — what befits a person of good standing in public. Kata taxin (according to order) is a military and organizational term. Both words come from the same civic and social world Paul and his readers inhabited.
